The wind shifted direction twice in one afternoon. The older folk say that means something.
Rain came down so hard the drainage channels overflowed and flooded the lower storage.
It was not the cold that got to people. It was the damp. Everything felt wet for days even after the skies cleared.
The fog stayed low and did not burn off until well past midday. You could not see ten paces ahead on the road.
Lightning struck the watchtower and split the beam clean through. Nobody was up there, thankfully.
We had three clear days in a row, which was enough for the builders to finish the outer wall. Then the rain came back like it was making up for lost time.
The hail was the size of river stones. Put holes in every canvas awning in the market square.
A dust storm blew through the pass and coated everything in a fine grit. Took a week to clean the well filters.
The temperature dropped so fast the puddles froze while people were still outside working.
There was a stillness before it hit. No breeze, no birdsong. Then the sky turned the color of a bruise and it all came down at once.
The dry spell lasted long enough that the reservoir dropped below the intake line. Rationing started on the third week.
A warm front rolled in from the south and melted the frost overnight. Turned every slope into a mudslide.
The clouds sat on the ridge for five days straight. No rain, just gray. People started getting short-tempered from the gloom.
We lost two rooftops to the gust that came through just before dawn. Nobody heard it coming because the wind was already loud.
The weather has been wrong all season. Warm when it should be cold, dry when it should be wet. The farmers do not know what to plant.
There was a ring around the moon three nights running. The sentries say that means heavy weather within the week.
The storm passed to the north of us but we could see it the whole time. Walls of rain moving across the valley like a curtain being drawn.
